codeSnapshot.label: mixed_precision.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.cuda.amp import GradScaler, autocast

model = nn.Linear(1024, 1024).cuda()
optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
scaler = GradScaler()  # for loss scaling (fp16 only)

# FP16 training with AMP (Automatic Mixed Precision)
for step in range(100):
    x = torch.randn(64, 1024, device='cuda')
    y = torch.randn(64, 1024, device='cuda')
    optimizer.zero_grad()

    # Forward pass in fp16
    with autocast(dtype=torch.float16):
        out = model(x)
        loss = nn.MSELoss()(out, y)

    # Backward pass: scale loss to prevent fp16 underflow
    scaler.scale(loss).backward()

    # Unscale gradients, clip, then step
    scaler.unscale_(optimizer)
    torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 1.0)
    scaler.step(optimizer)   # skips step if nan/inf detected
    scaler.update()          # adjust loss scale dynamically

# BF16 training (no loss scaling needed — wider exponent range)
for step in range(100):
    x = torch.randn(64, 1024, device='cuda', dtype=torch.bfloat16)
    y = torch.randn(64, 1024, device='cuda', dtype=torch.bfloat16)
    optimizer.zero_grad()

    with autocast(dtype=torch.bfloat16):
        out = model(x)
        loss = nn.MSELoss()(out, y)

    loss.backward()  # no scaler needed for bf16
    torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 1.0)
    optimizer.step()

# Memory comparison
print(f"fp32 param memory: {model.weight.element_size() * model.weight.numel() / 1e6:.1f} MB")
fp16_model = model.half()
print(f"fp16 param memory: {fp16_model.weight.element_size() * fp16_model.weight.numel() / 1e6:.1f} MB")

eli3: Neural networks normally use 32-bit floating point numbers (FP32) for all computations. Mixed precision training uses 16-bit floats (FP16 or BF16) for the forward and backward passes, and 32-bit floats only for weight updates. This uses half the memory and runs 2–4× faster on modern GPUs that have optimised 16-bit hardware.

The challenge with 16-bit: the range of representable numbers is much smaller than 32-bit. Very small gradient values underflow to zero and are lost. Loss scaling fixes this: multiply the loss by a large number (like 8192) before backprop, so gradients are also scaled up (they stay representable), then divide back before the weight update.

BF16 (Brain Float 16) has a wider exponent range than FP16, so it doesn't need loss scaling. LLaMA, GPT-4, and most modern LLMs are trained in BF16 — it provides the speed of FP16 without the underflow risk.

whenWhy.title: Training large models on GPU (standard for LLMs)
whenWhy.body: A 7B parameter model in FP32 uses 28GB GPU memory just for weights. In BF16: 14GB. With optimizer states (AdamW needs 2× weights in FP32): FP32 = 28+56=84GB; BF16 (BF16 weights + FP32 optimizer) = 14+56=70GB. All major LLM training pipelines (Megatron-LM, DeepSpeed, HuggingFace Accelerate) default to BF16 or FP16 mixed precision for 2× memory and 2–4× throughput improvement.

whenWhy.title: NVIDIA Tensor Cores acceleration
whenWhy.body: Modern NVIDIA GPUs (A100, H100, RTX 30/40 series) have Tensor Core units that perform FP16/BF16 matrix multiplications at 2–8× the throughput of FP32. A100: 77.6 TFLOPS FP16 vs 19.5 TFLOPS FP32. Mixed precision training uses Tensor Cores for the expensive matrix multiplications (attention, FFN) while keeping accumulation in FP32.

whenWhy.title: FP8 training (cutting edge)
whenWhy.body: H100 and H800 GPUs support FP8 (8-bit float) computation at 2× FP16 speed. NVIDIA's Transformer Engine (used in Megatron-LM) automatically selects FP8 for matrix multiplications and FP16/BF16 for other operations. FP8 training requires per-tensor scaling factors and careful numerical handling — currently only supported in specialised training frameworks.

deepDiveIntro: Mixed precision training (Micikevicius et al., 2018) keeps a full-precision master copy of weights for updates while running the forward and backward passes in 16-bit precision. FP16 uses five bits for the exponent and ten for the mantissa, giving a maximum representable value of about 65,504 and a minimum positive value around six ten-thousandths. Gradient values smaller than this minimum underflow silently to zero. Loss scaling multiplies the loss by a large constant before backpropagation, shifting gradients upward into the representable range; the gradients are divided back before the weight update. [1]

BF16 (Brain Float 16) uses eight exponent bits — the same as FP32 — but only seven mantissa bits. This gives it the same numerical range as FP32 so underflow and overflow cannot occur, eliminating the need for loss scaling entirely. A100 and H100 GPUs run BF16 matrix multiplications at the same throughput as FP16, making BF16 the preferred format for large language model training. [1]

deepDive.heading: Floating Point Formats
deepDive.body: FP32 provides the largest range and precision, with eight exponent bits and twenty-three mantissa bits. FP16 cuts both to five exponent bits and ten mantissa bits, reducing range dramatically while doubling the compute throughput on Tensor Core hardware. BF16 keeps the eight exponent bits of FP32 but reduces the mantissa to seven bits, trading some numerical precision for the full FP32 range — the best choice when overflow or underflow are the main concerns, as they are in LLM training. For typical model weights and gradients, the mantissa precision of BF16 is sufficient. [1]

deepDive.heading: Loss Scaling for FP16
deepDive.body: FP16 cannot represent gradient values smaller than about six times ten to the negative fifth; smaller values flush to zero and are lost. Loss scaling prevents this by multiplying the loss by a large constant before backpropagation, which shifts all gradients upward by the same factor into the representable range. The gradients are divided back before the optimizer step. PyTorch's GradScaler starts with a large scale factor and halves it whenever any gradient overflows to infinity, then gradually doubles it when training runs cleanly for many steps in a row. [1]

deepDive.heading: Memory Breakdown for Mixed Precision
deepDive.body: Surprisingly, the weight and gradient memory in mixed precision is not much smaller than full precision because FP32 master weights and FP32 Adam states are still required alongside the FP16 working copies. The real memory savings come from activations stored for the backward pass: transformers store many large activation tensors, and halving their precision from FP32 to FP16 cuts activation memory in half. With gradient checkpointing, activation memory is the bottleneck, so FP16 activations drive most of the practical benefit. [1]

deepDive.heading: Flash Attention and FP16
deepDive.body: Flash Attention (Dao et al., 2022) computes attention in FP16 or BF16 using tiling to fit intermediate results into on-chip SRAM, avoiding the quadratic memory allocation of standard attention implementations. Flash Attention 2 achieves two to four times the throughput of the standard implementation in BF16. The seven-bit mantissa of BF16 is sufficient for the softmax and weighted sum operations given the stabilizing effect of subsequent layer normalization and residual connections. BF16 combined with Flash Attention is now the standard configuration for training large language models.

youtubeLabel: Mixed Precision Training — FP16 vs BF16 Explained
takeaways: FP16/BF16 forward+backward; FP32 weight updates — 2× memory, 2–4× throughput. [1]
FP16 needs loss scaling (GradScaler) to prevent gradient underflow; BF16 does not. [1]
BF16 = FP32 exponent range + 7-bit mantissa — preferred for LLM training on A100/H100. [1]
Tensor Cores (NVIDIA A100): 77.6 FP16 TFLOPS vs 19.5 FP32 TFLOPS — 4× speed. [1]
FP8 (H100) provides 2× FP16 speed but requires per-tensor scaling — specialised frameworks only. [1]

edgeCases.q: When does loss scaling fail and cause NaN training?
edgeCases.a: Two causes of NaN in mixed precision: (1) Gradient overflow: gradient values > 65504 in FP16 become inf. GradScaler detects this and skips the update, halving the loss scale. If overflow is frequent (scale keeps halving), the final scale may be too small → underflow. Fix: reduce learning rate or use BF16. (2) Forward pass overflow: activations > 65504. This is harder to detect — GradScaler doesn't help here. Use autocast carefully: layernorm softmax must be computed in FP32. PyTorch's autocast does this automatically for standard layers, but custom kernels may not.

edgeCases.q: Should optimizer states be stored in FP32 or BF16?
edgeCases.a: AdamW's momentum (m) and second moment (v) are typically stored in FP32 even with BF16 training. The second moment v accumulates squared gradients over thousands of steps — precision errors compound. A BF16 v that accumulates 1000 steps of small gradients will have significant rounding error compared to FP32. Some frameworks (bitsandbytes) use 8-bit quantised optimizer states (LOMO, GALORE) to reduce memory further, with quantisation noise managed carefully. The hybrid: FP32 optimizer states + BF16 model weights is the standard for most LLM training.

sources.label: Micikevicius et al. (2018) — Mixed Precision Training, ICLR 2018 arXiv:1710.03740
